activities.outcome: Enhanced listening and speaking skills in learners. Learners' thoughts and self-expression promoted. Enhanced teacher to learner and learner to learner interpersonal relationships.
steps: Teacher greets the learners and talks about something that will draw their attention (e.g., something that happened to the teacher on the way to school, an event like a wedding, market, or festival).
Teacher speaks with proper intonation, diction, and voice modulation.
Teacher speaks in complete sentences.
After concluding, teacher asks learners to share their own experience.

activities.outcome: Enhanced listening and speaking skills in learners. Learners' thoughts and self-expression promoted. Enhanced teacher to learner and learner to learner interpersonal relationships.
steps: Teacher narrates a fictitious story from a book or magazines.
Teacher speaks with proper intonation, diction, and voice modulation.
Teacher speaks in complete sentences.
After finishing, the teacher asks learners: 'How was the story?', 'How did I tell the story?' (Discuss clarity of voice, full sentences etc.), 'Who will tell a story like me?'.
Then the teacher asks learners to share similar stories.

activities.outcome: Ability to observe, think, imagine, and express their thoughts with confidence.
steps: Teacher displays a picture card, conversation poster, or calendar.
Teacher mentions one item he or she sees in the picture as an example to learners.
Teacher asks learners to say something they see in the picture, ensuring no repetitions.
Teacher asks other learners to say a full sentence based on what is happening in the picture once a few learners have mentioned some items (this step can also be done on subsequent days).
Teacher asks a few learners to tell a short story based on the picture.

Alternatively, use the picture card or conversation poster to start a discussion on something shown that the learners can relate to (e.g., local markets, sports, festivals) (suitable for simple paragraph and Story Level).
Alternatively, use the picture to tell a story and ask learners to do the same.
Note: It is not necessary to do all steps in one day.

activities.outcome: Enhanced understanding of spelling rules. Improved understanding of grammar rules in sentence structure. Improved handwriting.
steps: Teacher says one, two, or three simple sentences depending on the level of learners.
Teacher writes the simple sentences on the chalkboard whilst saying them.
Teacher reads the sentences one by one whilst pointing at each word.
Teacher asks learners one at a time to read the sentences while pointing at the words.
Teacher asks the learners to write sentences in their exercise books.
Teacher pays attention to handwriting, spellings, and punctuation marks.
Teacher asks learners to check each other's exercise books and underline any mistakes (this can be done in 5 minutes).
Teacher checks handwriting, spelling, and punctuation marks.
Teacher gets feedback from one or two participants on their partner's performance.

activities.outcome: To improve handwriting. To correct spellings and understand the spelling rules. To understand punctuation marks and its significance in sentence structure. To understand grammar rules.
steps: Teacher asks children to listen carefully and write the words/sentences/a paragraph in their notebooks, reminding them to pay attention, write nicely with no spelling mistakes and with punctuation marks.
Teacher reads words/sentences/a paragraph with proper intonation, diction and voice modulation.
Teacher reads each word/sentence twice as a word/a sentence without decoding it.
Once learners complete writing, teacher asks them to exchange notebooks with their neighbours.
Subsequently, the teacher writes words/sentences/a paragraph on the board.
Teacher asks learners to check the handwriting, spelling and punctuation marks.
Teacher asks for feedback: How did their partner do?
Those learners who have few or no mistakes and those who found more mistakes are announced as the stars of the activity.

activities.outcome: Listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills building. Help children think about a word and create related words. Expanded vocabulary. Improved sentence construction leading to independent writing.
steps: Teacher asks learners to say one sound/syllable/word that comes to their mind.
Teacher writes all the sounds/syllables/words on the chalkboard and then asks which one they like most.
Teacher erases other sounds/syllables/words and only retains the selected one, then circles it and asks learners to mention one word associated with the circled sound/syllable/word.
Teacher asks each learner to say one word associated with the sound/syllable/word.
Teacher encourages learners to write on the chalkboard.
Teacher explains that learners can create sentences from each word when dealing with story level learners.

Note for Beginner and Letter level, Word and Simple Paragraph learners: follow processes mentioned above. For Story level learners, teacher can ask them to practice making sentences and form a simple paragraph or two.

Example of Mind Map steps: 1) Write a topic (e.g., 'fruit' or 'transportation'). 2) Encourage learners to say words they know associated with the topic. 3) Write all words learners say on the board (even if not closely associated). 4) If learners struggle, provide examples. 5) Write the word on the board and say it as you write it. 6) Go through each word again slowly, pointing and asking learners to repeat. 7) Ask learners to copy all words into exercise books; teacher walks around to ensure they do the exercise.

activities.outcome: Enhanced fluency through correct reading and pronunciation.
steps: Teacher selects one simple paragraph from the booklet and asks learners to turn to the page, ensuring all are on the same page.
Teacher gives instructions: 'I am going to read this paragraph. Listen to me carefully and look at the words I am reading.'
Teacher reads the text in a clear voice with proper diction, intonation, voice modulation, and pronunciation while pointing at each word.
Teacher makes sure every learner points a finger at each word being read aloud.
Teacher asks, 'How did I read?' (discussion on model reading) and then 'Is there anyone who can read as I did?'
Teacher points at one learner to read while making sure others follow by pointing a finger at every word.
Teacher leads a discussion on 'reading ability' of self and all learners who read.

activities.outcome: Learners identify words that rhyme.
steps: Choose a word card from the deck and put it on the chalkboard.
Ask learners to say words that rhyme with the given word.

activities.outcome: Learners match pictures with words.
steps: Place words and their picture cards face down on a table.
Let learners come to the front and flip cards to see if they can match the picture with the word.
Repeat the activity with different learners.
Avoid using more than 8 cards to keep activity short and engaging.

activities.outcome: Learners recognize words and letters.
materials_needed: Word bank (12-15 words written on board)
steps: Give each learner 9 stones/bottle tops and allow them to draw a tic-tac-toe grid on their slate/card box.
Ask learners to fill the nine spaces with words from a word bank given on the board (must have between 12 and 15 words).
Let one person come up and choose a word from the bag and let learners put an Oware bead on their board if they have the letter.
The first one to get 3 horizontally, vertically or diagonally wins.

activities.outcome: Learners quickly recognize and say words.
steps: Gather word cards in one hand and show the words quickly.
Let learners say the words as you flash each card.
Re-shuffle and change the order.

activities.outcome: Learners spell words based on pictures.
steps: Show the picture side of the word and then say it out loud.
Choose a learner to stand up and spell the word.
Let learners confirm whether the word was spelled correctly.

activities.outcome: To familiarize children at beginner and letter with the pattern of letters, the sounds and all the syllables. To build the knowledge to create, read, and write words with the help of syllable activities.
steps: The facilitator tells children he/she will read the syllable chart, pointing a finger under each letter/syllable.
The facilitator reads the chart with clear diction, voice modulation and pronunciation – SLOWLY.
The facilitator reads the chart with vowels first and then reads all the consonants.
The facilitator tells children not to repeat after him/her and asks children to listen and see the activity carefully.
The facilitator reads the chart horizontally, vertically, pointing at each sound in a zigzag way and/or by hopping on different letters, vowels and syllables.
The facilitator asks children to practice in small groups and then individually.

Note: This activity takes around 15 minutes in the initial phase. Children can work in groups and adopt the process quickly. Group practice is important. Do not discourage children for wrong pronunciation.

activities.outcome: Learn phonemic sound of each syllable with its letter correlation. Learn to create words based on the syllables. Instead of memorizing they learn spelling rules automatically. Able to write words. Can create simple sentences.
steps: Teacher selects one word from the simple paragraph (e.g., 'mother').
The word is written prominently on the board.
Teacher decodes the word (e.g., m-other).
Learners read the letters, recognizing them and their sounds.
Teacher writes a string of at least two consonants with all vowels (e.g., 'm' with 'a, e, i, o, u' to form 'ma, me, mi, mo, mu').
Remaining letters can then be added, and learners are asked to create spelling based on the letters.
A group activity is conducted, and the group which makes more words is felicitated.

activities.outcome: Enhanced recall and recognition of letter sounds and syllables.
steps: Teacher prepares a basket containing letter sounds or syllables to be taught.
Learners stand or sit in a circle.
Teacher introduces a song to the learners.

Teacher explains instructions: The basket will be passed around the circle while the song is sung. When the song ends, the learner holding the basket picks a card and says the syllable or sound. The learner then says one word that has that syllable or sound. That word can be written on the board with the help of the syllabic chart.
The song is sung, and the basket is passed around.
Repeat this several times so that several learners can pick out sounds or syllables.

activities.outcome: Enhanced recall and recognition of letter sounds and syllables.
steps: Teacher chooses 5-6 sound and syllables Flashcards to introduce to the learners.
Teacher shows a Flashcard to learners and puts it upside down (face down) on the table/floor.
Teacher does not say the sound/syllable.
Teacher repeats for all Flashcards, placing them face down in a random manner.
Teacher points at a sound/syllable on the Syllabic Chart.
Teacher chooses a learner to find the Flashcard that corresponds to the sound/syllable on the Syllabic Chart.
Learner picks up a Flashcard and shows it to the teacher and class, teacher asks: 'Is this correct?'
If incorrect, teacher asks: 'What sound/syllable are we looking for? What sound/syllable is this?'
Incorrect Flashcard is put back in its position. Learner has up to three attempts. Once correct, learner matches it with the one the teacher pointed at and puts it back in the same position.
Teacher points to another sound/syllable on the syllabic chart, and chooses another learner to find that Flashcard.

activities.activity_name: Word-Building Game with Ending and Beginning Syllable
activities.outcome: Developed skill of identifying starting and ending syllables in words. Improved formation of words that start with specific sounds.
steps: Teacher divides the class into two groups.
Teacher asks the first group for a word, and writes it on the chalkboard.
Teacher asks the second group for a word that begins with the ending syllable of the first word. Repeats the process.
This can be a competition, with the group that makes the fewest mistakes in creating words being the winner.

activities.outcome: Learners form two or three syllable words.
steps: Place learners in groups of three or four and give each group a set of Syllable Cards.
Ask each the group to work together for about 5 minutes to form as many two or three syllable words as possible.
Ask each group to present their words.
Ask learners to count the number of words they have created. The winner is the group with the highest number of words.

activities.outcome: Learners match small and capital letters. Learners identify letter sounds. Learners form words from letters.
steps: Make small and capital letter cards (or use existing ones).
Distribute the cards to the learners.
Ask learners to find their partner (e.g., capital 'A' finds small 'a').
When partners are found, they stand together and sound their letter.
Write both small and capital letters on the chalkboard and ask the learners to copy them in their exercise books.
This can also be done with words: post a word on the chalkboard, have learners with different letters find each other to form the word, say their sounds, and blend the word together.

activities.outcome: Learners form words from syllables.
steps: Give each group a card with a grid of syllables (e.g., 'so', 'to', 'ka', 'ci', 'ti', 'ma', 'ci', 'ko', 'lo').
Make syllable cards for those syllables (e.g., 'ti', 'lo', 'ci' x2).
Give each group at least 9 syllable cards. Put your syllables in a bag. Pull them out one at a time.
The team that forms a word first shouts 'Bingo' and is the winner.
The game can be made more competitive with more words and several bingo cards.

activities.outcome: Improved construction of sentences in a logical order to tell a story.
steps: Teacher gives a sentence and writes it on the chalkboard.
Teacher asks learners to give sentences logically connected to the first sentence to make a story.
Teacher writes the sentences on the chalkboard as they are given by the learners.
Teacher reads the complete story to the class and then discusses it.
Teacher explains the procedure of coming up with a logical story.
Teacher divides learners into groups and asks them to appoint one leader in each group.
Teacher asks the group leader to say a sentence and write it down.
Teacher asks each group member to add a sentence in turn, which should be written down by the group leader.
When everyone has added their sentence, the group leader will read out the story to the whole class.
The class will reflect on each story (e.g., does it make sense? good sequence? characters?).
Based on reflections, the groups should rewrite their stories and read them to the class.

Important points: Ensure all sentences are in a similar context and have logical connections, story has a proper ending, tenses and punctuation marks are used properly, sequence of events is clear. If no end, ask group to form one.

activities.outcome: To help learners understand errors in the sentences. Build learners ability to understand grammar rules.
materials_needed: List of incorrect sentences (prepared by teacher)
steps: Teacher says a simple sentence that is grammatically correct.
Teacher writes the sentence on the board with some grammatical mistake.
Teacher reads the sentence which is written incorrectly on the board.
If learners say it is wrong or there is a mistake, teacher asks a learner to point out the mistake/s and to write the correct version.
The activity is repeated several times with other incorrect sentences.
Teacher divides children into small groups and gives a set of incorrect sentences on the board for groups to correct.
Note: Teacher can prepare lists of incorrect sentences with mistakes in gender, singular-plural, tenses, structure etc. for different sessions.

activities.outcome: Learners practice spelling words. Learners at Simple Paragraph and Story Levels can explain meanings of difficult words.
steps: Put learners in groups of three or four.
Pick a two or three-syllable word or a sight word previously taught (e.g., 'Amai') and ask: 'Kodi 'amai' ilembedwa motani?' (How is 'amai' written)
Give each group a few minutes to figure out how the word is spelled.
When time is up, pick a group at random to spell the word.
If the group spells correctly, ask them to write the word on the chalkboard. If incorrect, pick another group.
Repeat activity with ten words, starting with easy ones and then selecting longer/more difficult words.

activities.outcome: Learners create grammatically correct sentences. Learners understand sentence structure.
materials_needed: Word cards (SUBJECT, VERB, OBJECT, Preposition - optional)
steps: Make a pile of word cards for SUBJECT, VERB and OBJECT. Put the words on a table/floor and allow 2 or 3 learners to choose a card from each pile and create a sentence (ensure piles are jumbled).
When learners learn prepositions, add a pile of preposition cards.
Let the class confirm that the sentence made makes sense and let the teacher write it on the chalkboard.
If the sentence is wrong, let the class correct it.

activities.outcome: Learners read simple sentences. Learners engage in active reading.
steps: Write Simple Sentences (not more than five words) on pieces of plain paper (one sentence per paper).
Before learners arrive, fold papers and hide them around the classroom.
When learners arrive, explain that 'treasures' (sentences) are hidden.
Place learners in groups of three or four and explain they have a few minutes to find as many hidden treasures as they can.
When time is up, ask each group to come to the front and show the number of treasures found. Learners in each group take turns to read at least one treasure.

activities.outcome: Learners at this level can read fluently but have a problem with comprehension. Teacher uses lesson-based activity to follow all steps. Learners will be able to express themselves confidently. Writing and reading skill, logical, critical thinking.
steps: **STEP 1: Discussion on Title**: Write the title of the story and discuss it with the learners. Let them imagine what is in the story based on the title.

**STEP 2: Reading by the Teacher**: Give story cards or story books to the learners. Instruct learners not to read, but to listen first. Demonstrate reading the story with a clear voice, proper diction, punctuation, and correct pronunciation. Learners listen and point at the words as the teacher reads.

**STEP 3: Reading by the learners**: Taking turns, learners read the text (at least 2 to 3 sentences each). Ask learners if the content matches their imagination based on the title. Remind learners of the story content by asking them to: Remember the keywords (teacher writes on chalkboard), Rearrange the words as per sequence, Retell the story in their own words using keywords (teacher explains that narration is summary).

**STEP 4: Group Activities - Competition**: Teacher divides the class into groups and asks each group to prepare Quiz questions based on one of these activities: Activity 1: Facts from the story e.g., filling in the blanks or completing the sentence. Activity 2: Meaning of difficult words in the text. Activity 3: Words with opposite meanings. Activity 4: Words with the same meanings.

**STEP 5: Further Discussion on the Text**: Ask learners questions to think critically and analytically: 'What is the story about?', 'Why do you think this story was written?', 'What is your own experience in relation to this story?', 'Is there anything more that you would like to share about this story?'.

**STEP 6: Writing**: Learners are expected to write a summary of the text in their own words, or answer comprehension questions based on the story. Note: If time does not allow in the first lesson, it may end at summary. The lesson may be split into two days; the first day could stop after one or two group activities.

activities.outcome: Learners imagine and describe story settings. Learners develop drawing skills.
steps: Tell learners that as they listen to a story, they should try to imagine the scene or location in which the story takes place.
After reading, have them describe the scene in words while the teacher writes those descriptions on the board.
After descriptions are written, have learners draw out the scenes on a sheet of paper.
Choose two or three people to share their drawings with the class.

activities.outcome: Learners order sentences to form a sensible story. Learners identify punctuation marks and sentence types.
steps: Make sets of sentence strips of the same story (enough sets for each group). Each strip should have one sentence from the story.
Place learners in groups of two or three and provide each group with one set of sentence cards.
Give each group a few minutes to work together to correctly order the sentences of the story.
When time is up, pick a group and ask them to read the sentences as ordered. If incorrect, ask another group. Use the chalkboard if groups struggle.
Help learners identify the type of punctuation mark and sentence type.
Repeat the activity with about five short stories.
Note: Learners should re-arrange sentences in the correct order to make a sensible story.
